title: GDPR Right to Erasure Verification

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.17 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.17 (Right to erasure) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.11: GDPR Right to Erasure Verification.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.11' running on index=itsm (completed erasure requests), all indexed data (post-erasure verification scan), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=itsm` (completed erasure requests), all indexed data (post-erasure verification scan)
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928)
spl:
| inputlookup gdpr_completed_erasures.csv WHERE status="completed"
| eval erasure_date=strptime(completion_date, "%Y-%m-%d")
| eval days_since_erasure=round((now()-erasure_date)/86400, 0)
| where days_since_erasure >= 7 AND days_since_erasure <= 90
| map maxsearches=50 search="search index=* earliest=-90d \"$$subject_identifier$$\" | head 1 | eval subject_id=\"$$subject_identifier$$\", request_id=\"$$request_id$$\""
| where isnotnull(subject_id)
| table subject_id, request_id, index, sourcetype, host, _time
description: Article 17 gives data subjects the right to erasure ("right to be forgotten"). While UC-22.1.2 tracks the DSAR ticket lifecycle, this use case verifies that erasure was actually executed across all systems by searching for residual data subject identifiers after the erasure deadline — catching incomplete deletions before they become regulatory violations.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Export completed erasure requests to `gdpr_completed_erasures.csv` with subject identifiers (hashed email, user ID, etc.) and completion dates; (2) run weekly to search for residual occurrences; (3) alert the DPO on any matches — these indicate incomplete erasure; (4) track remediation tickets for each residual finding; (5) exclude Splunk indexes with legitimate legal-hold retention from the scan and document the exception. Indexes required: `index=itsm`.
visualization: Table (residual data findings), Single value (incomplete erasures), Bar chart (residual data by system), Timeline (findings over time). Key fields: `days_since_erasure`, `erasure_date`, `host`, `index`, `request_id`, `sourcetype`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Technical deletion propagation delays** — Erasure requests completed in primary systems may take 24-48 hours to propagate to downstream replicas, caches, and search indices; allow for the documented propagation window before flagging as incomplete.

2. **Backup system exclusions** — Data in offline or cold-tier backups is typically excluded from immediate erasure per the documented backup retention exception; the search may flag these as unerased records when they are scheduled for expiry with the backup rotation.

3. **Legal obligation retention** — Data that must be retained under other legal obligations (tax records, anti-money-laundering) overrides the right to erasure under Art.17(3)(b); verify the exemption basis against the retention schedule.

4. **Pseudonymised research data** — Data that has been pseudonymised for scientific research under Art.17(3)(d) exemption may appear as unerased when it is actually lawfully retained.

5. **Partial erasure for interconnected records** — Records that are interleaved with other data subjects' data may require redaction rather than full deletion; the verification check may flag partially-redacted records as incomplete erasure.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_completed_erasures.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_completed_erasures.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.17`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.17 (Right to erasure) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.11: GDPR Right to Erasure Verification." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Export completed erasure requests to `gdpr_completed_erasures.csv` with subject identifiers (hashed email, user ID, etc.) and completion dates. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 2:** run weekly to search for residual occurrences. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert the DPO on any matches — these indicate incomplete erasure. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** track remediation tickets for each residual finding. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** exclude Splunk indexes with legitimate legal-hold retention from the scan and document the exception. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Right to Erasure Verification," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search begins with `| inputlookup gdpr_completed_erasures.csv` — the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `gdpr_completed_erasures.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

• Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
• Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
• Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup gdpr_completed_erasures.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true gdpr_completed_erasures_audit.csv` — this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
• Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the last 90 days) is the detection trigger — when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.11: GDPR Right to Erasure Verification]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.11,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `days_since_erasure`, `erasure_date`, `host`, `index`, `request_id`, `sourcetype`, `subject_id`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, days_since_erasure, erasure_date, host, index, request_id, sourcetype, subject_id
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `days_since_erasure`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(days_since_erasure) as p50 perc90(days_since_erasure) as p90 perc99(days_since_erasure) as p99 max(days_since_erasure) as hi
```

The current threshold `7` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.11: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`residual data findings`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`incomplete erasures`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Distribution (`residual data by system`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 4 — Time trend (`findings over time`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of right to erasure verification — so we can show we look after private information the way the privacy rules that apply to us require.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Submit a test erasure request and verify that after the documented processing window (e.g., 48 hours), the verification search still finds the test record in a downstream system; confirm the alert identifies the incomplete erasure.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Submit a test erasure request, confirm deletion across all documented systems within the processing window, then run the verification search; confirm zero residual records are found and no alert fires.

evidence: Erasure verification report showing per-system deletion confirmation, propagation status, any exemptions applied under Art.17(3), and final completeness attestation.
exclusions: Data retained under Art.17(3) exemptions including legal obligation, public health, archiving in public interest, or legal claims defence with documented exemption basis per record.
